title: Courgette bread
subtitle: A tender quick loaf with grated courgette and lemon
excerpt: Courgette bread is a quick loaf leavened with baking powder, not yeast. Grated courgette vanishes into the crumb as it bakes, leaving moisture and a slight earthiness behind. A strip of lemon zest keeps it bright.
sourceNotes: Courgette quick bread is a North American tradition that reached British home kitchens in the early 2000s. The technique follows the standard muffin method: combine dry ingredients, combine wet ingredients, fold together without overmixing. The recipe here is synthesised from the established British home-baking canon with a lemon note added for brightness.

recipe.freezeNotes: Slice the cooled loaf, freeze in a sealed bag for up to 3 months. Thaw at room temperature for an hour or toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The loaf keeps well wrapped for two days at room temperature and improves slightly on day two.

glossaryTerms.definition: The muffin method keeps quick breads tender by preventing gluten development. You mix the dry ingredients in one bowl, the wet in another, then fold them together with as few strokes as possible. A lumpy batter is correct. Overmixing activates gluten and produces a dense, tunnelled loaf.

content.text: : dry ingredients in one bowl, wet in another, fold together and bake. There is no yeast, no kneading, and no fermentation time. The courgette provides moisture so the loaf stays tender for two days without going dry. Grate it coarsely and leave it unsqueezed; that water is part of the recipe.
A metal loaf tin conducts heat more evenly than glass. Line it with baking paper or grease it well; the loaf will cling to an unlined tin.
Heat the oven to 180 degrees C (fan or conventional). Grease a 900 g loaf tin and line with a strip of baking paper that overhangs the two long sides.
Mix the dry and wet separately

In a large bowl, whisk together the 300 g plain flour, 2 tsp baking powder, half a teaspoon bicarbonate of soda, 1 tsp ground cinnamon and half a teaspoon salt. In a second bowl, beat the 2 eggs, then stir in the 100 ml vegetable oil, 150 g caster sugar and the lemon zest. Fold in the 200 g grated courgette.

Pour the wet mixture into the dry and fold together with a spatula. Stop as soon as no dry flour remains. The batter will be thick and slightly lumpy. Scrape into the prepared tin and level the top lightly.

Bake for 50 to 55 minutes until a skewer inserted into the centre comes out clean and the top is a deep golden brown with a crack running along the length. If the top is colouring too fast, lay a piece of foil loosely over it after 35 minutes. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then lift out using the paper overhang and transfer to a wire rack for at least 20 minutes before slicing.

items.problem: Loaf is dense and gummy in the middle
items.solution: It underbaked. The courgette releases steam slowly, so the centre needs time to set. Test with a skewer at 50 minutes; if it comes out wet, give it 5 more minutes and test again. An oven thermometer helps confirm the temperature is correct.

items.problem: Loaf rose well but sank as it cooled
items.solution: The batter was overmixed, which developed too much gluten. Fold just until the flour disappears on the next bake. A few small lumps in the batter are fine and actually desirable.
This is normal and expected. The crack forms as the outside sets before the centre finishes rising. It's a sign of a well-made quick loaf, not a fault.
